target.name: Rain Industries
target.founder: Bryan Hatton, Ephraim Nowak, Maxwell Brodie

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $15M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Rain Industries is a Seed-stage drone and autonomous systems company founded by Bryan Hatton, Ephraim Nowak, and Maxwell Brodie that has raised $14.7M to date. The Drones & Autonomous sector includes seven private peers—notably Joby Aviation, Nuro, Archer Aviation, Elroy Air, and Thoron—though no public comparables exist in this narrowly defined space.

Given the early stage and limited peer visibility, a Stage-Progression approach provides the primary valuation signal. Using total capital raised ($14.7M) as a proxy for current value, applying a typical 2.5x multiplier for the Seed-to-next-stage transition yields an implied valuation of $37M. A Peer-Median approach was not applicable; while four same-sector peers exist in the market, none met minimum thresholds (three or more datapoints) at matching stage or adjacent stage, making sector-median comparables insufficiently reliable.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $31M to $42M, centered on $37M. The range reflects ±15% variance around the point estimate to account for execution risk and fundraising conditions. Key uncertainties include the absence of close-stage comparables for calibration, early revenue/metrics visibility, and the developing nature of the autonomous vehicle and drone markets. Additional data on company-specific milestones, burn rate, and commercial traction would sharpen the valuation in subsequent rounds.
